examples.description: Watercolor landscape with blues and greens

expertChoice.reasoning: Watercolors are delicate — the frame should never compete. Natural wood adds warmth without weight. The mat is warm-toned to complement the earthy greens rather than the dominant blue.

commonMistakes: Using a bright white mat — kills the warmth of the watercolor
Using a heavy ornate frame — overwhelms the lightness of the medium
Matching mat to the blue — makes it look like a blue painting instead of a landscape
Using a wide dark frame — too much visual weight for a delicate medium

examples.description: Bold abstract painting with reds and blacks

expertChoice.moulding: Simple black float frame, medium profile (1.5 inch)
expertChoice.mat: No mat — float frame shows canvas edge
expertChoice.reasoning: Abstract art this bold should not be confined by a mat. The float frame creates a clean gallery presentation. Black frame provides a boundary without competing with the reds.

commonMistakes: Adding a mat to canvas — canvas should show its edges
Using an ornate gold frame — clashes with modern abstract energy
Using a red mat to match the reds — doubles down on intensity instead of containing it
Using a thin frame — the drama of this piece needs a frame with some presence

examples.description: Classical oil portrait with warm earth tones

expertChoice.moulding: Antique gold with carved profile, wide (2.5 inch)
expertChoice.mat: Double mat — outer cream, inner burgundy reveal
expertChoice.reasoning: Classical portraits demand presence. The antique gold frame matches the era and warmth. Double mat with burgundy inner reveal adds formal depth and picks up the warm undertones. Wide frame gives the portrait stature.

commonMistakes: Using a thin modern frame — diminishes the gravitas of a classical portrait
Using bright shiny gold instead of antiqued — looks cheap and gaudy
Single white mat — too plain for formal portraiture
Matching mat to the dominant brown — muddy and flat

expertChoice.mat: Single mat, white with generous margins (3.5 inches)
expertChoice.reasoning: Gallery-standard photography framing: thin black frame, wide white mat. The generous mat gives the photo room to breathe and creates a gallery-quality presentation. The frame is nearly invisible — the viewer sees the photograph, not the frame.

commonMistakes: Using a decorative or ornate frame — fights the documentary quality
Narrow mat — photos need room to breathe
Colored mat — distracts from the tonal qualities of B&W
Wide frame — overwhelms the photograph

expertChoice.moulding: Warm antiqued gold with gentle scoop profile, medium-wide (2 inch)
expertChoice.mat: Double mat — outer cream, inner sage green reveal
expertChoice.reasoning: Impressionist garden scenes glow with warm antiqued gold frames. The double mat adds sophistication — the sage green inner reveal picks up the garden foliage without matching the dominant purple. The warm cream outer mat complements the golden light in the painting.

commonMistakes: Matching mat to the purple — overpowers the garden atmosphere
Using a cold silver frame — kills the warmth of impressionist light
Using a stark white mat — too clinical for the warmth of the scene
Modern minimalist frame — wrong era, wrong energy

expertChoice.mat: Single white mat, extra-wide margins (4 inches)
expertChoice.reasoning: Minimalist art needs minimalist framing. The thin white frame nearly disappears. Extra-wide mat margins give the geometric forms maximum space to exist. Everything is clean, precise, and breathing.

commonMistakes: Any ornate or decorative frame — completely wrong aesthetic
Narrow mat — minimalist art needs space around it
Dark frame — adds visual weight where none is wanted
Double mat — too much complexity for minimal art
